paragraphs.text: A party of men engaged in craw-fishing on San Nicolas island relate to the San Diego Union a strange tale of earthquakes, winds, storms and meteorological disturbances which occurred there about the first of July.
Coral harbor, the only little harbor possessed by the island, was completely filled up with sand during these disturbances. Speaking of these islands, the San Diego Union says:

"San Nicolas is the most westerly of the group known as the Santa Barbara islands, and is about 150 miles distant from San Diego. Twenty-five years ago it was covered with grass, which afforded pasture for thousands of sheep, and its owners lived there in a fine residence.

"A strange transformation has been going on since then. It is now a windy, barren, sandy waste, where but a few hundred sheep, without a shepherd, eke out a miserable existence. It is but eight miles long and four wide, but wherever one digs can be found human bones and skulls. In the distant past it was either densely populated, or a burial ground, or a great battlefield.

["]Some fifty years ago the last inhabitant was found in the person of an old woman. A Santa Barbara otter hunter named Nidever found her, and took her to his home, where she died shortly after, without being able to make any one understand the language. She was found in a cave, subsisting on fish which she caught with hooks made out of abalone shells. Her discovery caused considerable interest in the scientific world at the time.
"Captain Mullet, who had visited the islands many times during the past twenty years, says he was glad to get away from it with his men, and has visited it for the last time."
